skill.name: threat-mitigation-mapping
skill.description: Map security threats to layered controls, identify mitigation gaps, and build remediation roadmaps for risk treatment planning.
skill.summary: Connect threats to practical security controls and mitigation plans.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk indicators, but review found they are security-domain vocabulary, Markdown code fences, diagrams, and documentation examples. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential access, data exfiltration, or executable installer behavior was found. The remaining risk is low because the skill includes Python examples and links to external security references.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Documentation Markers
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported backtick executions are Markdown fences, inline resource references, and diagrams. They do not invoke shell commands or run code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious characters are Markdown formatting around examples and links. There is no script entry point, shell invocation, or user input passed to a command.

low_findings.title: Security Vocabulary Triggered Malware Keyword Rules
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Terms such as SOC2, DDoS, encryption, threat categories, and unmapped threats appear in a defensive control mapping playbook.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text describes defensive controls, compliance references, and risk planning. I found no command-and-control behavior, weak crypto implementation, or scanning instructions.

low_findings.title: External Reference Links
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW: The playbook links to NIST, CIS Controls, and MITRE D3FEND. These are documentation links, not automated network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible reference links to recognized security resources. They create disclosure value but do not indicate exfiltration or hidden network activity.

content.value_statement: Security teams often know their threats but lack a clear control coverage map. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code turn threats into prioritized mitigation plans.
actual_capabilities: Maps threat categories to preventive, detective, and corrective controls.
Identifies mitigation gaps and weak defense-in-depth coverage.
Prioritizes remediation work by impact, likelihood, coverage, and cost.
Builds control implementation roadmaps from existing threat lists.
Supports control effectiveness review using structured test planning.
limitations: It does not replace a formal risk assessment or compliance audit.
It depends on accurate threat, asset, and control inventory inputs.
It does not run security tools or validate systems directly.
Its sample code needs adaptation before production use.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Remediation Work
use_cases.description: Turn a threat register into a ranked list of controls and implementation phases.

use_cases.title: Review Architecture Coverage
use_cases.description: Check whether an application architecture has layered controls for important threat categories.

use_cases.title: Prepare Control Validation
use_cases.description: Create a focused test plan for confirming that selected controls reduce risk.

prompt_templates.title: Map Basic Threats
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner mapping for a small system
prompt_templates.prompt: Map these threats to practical security controls. Include control type, layer, and a short reason for each recommendation: [paste threats].

prompt_templates.title: Find Coverage Gaps
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this threat list and current controls. Identify unmapped threats, missing layers, and the highest priority gaps: [paste details].

prompt_templates.title: Build a Roadmap
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a phased mitigation roadmap from these threats, risks, costs, and constraints. Explain the priority order clearly: [paste inputs].

prompt_templates.title: Validate Control Effectiveness
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a control effectiveness validation plan for these mitigations. Include test objectives, expected evidence, owners, and review cadence: [paste controls].

output_examples.input: A web application faces spoofing, injection, and denial-of-service threats.
output_examples.output: The output groups controls by threat, recommends MFA, input validation, WAF, rate limiting, and monitoring, then flags missing detective controls.

output_examples.input: A team has a threat register and a partial control inventory.
output: A coverage summary that shows mapped and unmapped threats.
A gap list with priority levels and suggested owners.
A phased roadmap for the most important control improvements.

output_examples.input: An auditor asks how control effectiveness will be validated.
output_examples.output: The output defines test objectives, expected evidence, pass criteria, and review frequency for each selected control.

best_practices: Provide threat categories, business impact, likelihood, and existing controls before asking for mapping.
Ask for preventive, detective, and corrective controls to avoid single-layer coverage.
Review recommendations with system owners before treating them as final remediation commitments.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill with vague threats that lack affected assets or business impact.
Do not treat sample control lists as complete for every environment.
Do not skip validation planning after selecting mitigations.

faq.question: Does this skill run security scans?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning guidance and examples for mapping threats to controls.

faq.question: Can it support compliance work?
faq.answer: Yes. It can align controls to common references, but it is not a compliance certification tool.

faq.question: What input works best?
faq.answer: A threat list, affected assets, risk ratings, existing controls, budget limits, and implementation constraints work best.

faq.question: Can it help with defense in depth?
faq.answer: Yes. It checks whether controls cover multiple layers and control types.

faq.question: Does it provide production-ready code?
faq.answer: No. The included code is illustrative and needs review before operational use.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Security architects, risk managers, GRC analysts, and engineering leaders can use it for mitigation planning.
